title: Demand and Supply
summary:
## Big picture

A **market** is any arrangement that brings buyers and sellers together. In a free market, the interaction of **demand** and **supply** determines the **equilibrium price** and **quantity** — this is the *price mechanism*, which performs three functions: **rationing** (high prices ration scarce goods), **signalling** (prices signal where resources are wanted), and **incentive** (high prices reward producers to supply more). This chapter is the foundation for almost everything else in microeconomics.

## Demand

**Demand** = the quantity of a good consumers are *willing and able* to buy at each price in a given period. Note *effective demand* (backed by ability to pay), not just desire.

**Law of demand:** as price rises, quantity demanded falls (and vice versa), *ceteris paribus*. The demand curve slopes **downward**.

Why it slopes down:
- **Income effect** — a price fall raises real income, so consumers can buy more.
- **Substitution effect** — a price fall makes the good cheaper relative to substitutes, so consumers switch to it.
- **Diminishing marginal utility** — each extra unit gives less satisfaction, so consumers only buy more at a lower price.

### Movement vs shift (examiners test this constantly)
- A change in the good's **own price** → a **movement along** the curve (*extension* if Q rises, *contraction* if Q falls).
- A change in **any other factor** → a **shift** of the whole curve.

**Conditions of demand (shift factors) — mnemonic PIRATES:**
- **P**opulation / demographics
- **I**ncome (normal good: ↑income → ↑demand; inferior good: ↑income → ↓demand)
- **R**elated goods — substitutes (↑price of rival → ↑demand for this) and complements (↑price of complement → ↓demand)
- **A**dvertising / tastes & fashion
- **T**axes & subsidies on consumers
- **E**xpectations of future prices
- **S**easons / weather

## Supply

**Supply** = the quantity producers are *willing and able* to sell at each price in a given period.

**Law of supply:** as price rises, quantity supplied rises, *ceteris paribus*. The supply curve slopes **upward** because higher prices mean higher potential profit (an incentive to produce more) and cover the rising marginal cost of extra output.

### Conditions of supply (shift factors) — mnemonic PINTSWC:
- **P**roductivity
- **I**ndirect taxes (↑tax → supply shifts left) and **subsidies** (shift right)
- **N**umber of firms
- **T**echnology
- **S**ubsidies / costs of production (raw materials, wages, energy)
- **W**eather / shocks (esp. agriculture)
- **C**osts of factors of production

Again: own-price change → **movement** along supply; any other factor → **shift**.

## Market equilibrium

**Equilibrium** is where demand = supply (the curves cross): the market *clears*, with no tendency to change. Price here is the **equilibrium / market-clearing price**.

- If price is **above** equilibrium → **surplus** (excess supply) → downward pressure on price.
- If price is **below** equilibrium → **shortage** (excess demand) → upward pressure on price.

The market self-corrects back to equilibrium — the price mechanism at work.

### Working through shifts (the exam skill)
Always state the *direction* each curve moves, then the effect on **price and quantity**:

| Change | Curve | New price | New quantity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rise in consumer income (normal good) | D right | ↑ | ↑ |
| New rival product launched | D left | ↓ | ↓ |
| Fall in raw-material costs | S right | ↓ | ↑ |
| New tax on producers | S left | ↑ | ↓ |

> If **both** curves shift, the effect on either price *or* quantity is **indeterminate** — you can sign one but not the other without knowing the relative size of the shifts. Examiners love this.

## Real-world application

- **Supply shocks:** the 2022 spike in global **wheat and energy prices** (Russia–Ukraine war) shifted the **supply** of food and fuel left worldwide, raising prices from Europe to North Africa.
- **Demand shifts:** young, growing populations in economies like **India and Nigeria** keep **demand** for housing and food shifting right, while ageing populations (e.g. **Japan**) push some demands the other way.
- **Both curves at once:** the global **oil market** — OPEC+ supply cuts alongside reviving **Chinese** demand push price up but leave the change in quantity ambiguous.

## Why it matters

Demand and supply analysis underlies elasticities, government intervention (price controls, taxes, subsidies), and the whole AD/AS macro model. Master shifting curves cleanly now and the rest of the course gets much easier.

keyTerms.definition: Demand backed by the willingness AND ability to pay, not mere desire.
‘All other things equal’ — the assumption that lets us isolate the effect of one variable.
A movement along the demand curve caused by a change in the good's own price (extension = more demanded, contraction = less).
A good whose demand rises as consumer income rises (positive income elasticity).
A good whose demand falls as income rises (negative income elasticity).
Goods consumed instead of one another; a rise in the price of one raises demand for the other.
Goods consumed together; a rise in the price of one lowers demand for the other.
The price at which quantity demanded equals quantity supplied and the market clears.
When price is below equilibrium so quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied.
When price is above equilibrium so quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded.
The way prices ration, signal, and incentivise to allocate resources in a market.

diagrams: **Basic D/S equilibrium:** axes Price (vertical) and Quantity (horizontal); downward D, upward S; mark equilibrium price P* and quantity Q* at the intersection. Always label axes, curves, and the equilibrium point.
**Demand shift:** show D1 → D2 (right or left) along a fixed S, then mark the new equilibrium and use arrows to show P and Q changing. Never confuse a shift with a movement.
**Supply shift:** show S1 → S2 along a fixed D; show new P and Q. Tax shifts S left/up, subsidy shifts S right/down.
**Shortage / surplus:** draw a horizontal price line above (surplus) or below (shortage) equilibrium and label the horizontal gap between the curves as excess supply / excess demand.
tips: **Always label diagrams fully** — axes (Price, Quantity), every curve (D, S, D1, D2…), and the equilibrium price & quantity. Unlabelled diagrams lose easy marks.
Use the precise command-word language: *‘the demand curve shifts to the right from D1 to D2’*, not ‘demand goes up’. **Shift vs movement** is the single most common error.
For ‘analyse/explain’ questions, **chain your logic**: cause → which curve shifts and why → new equilibrium → effect on price AND quantity.
Watch the **own-price trap**: a change in the good's own price NEVER shifts its own demand or supply curve — it's a movement along.
When **both curves shift**, state clearly that the effect on price *or* quantity is **indeterminate** and explain it depends on the relative magnitude of the shifts — this scores evaluation marks.
Bring in **real, varied international examples** (global wheat/oil price shocks; population growth in India or Nigeria; housing markets) for application and evaluation marks.
Distinguish a normal from an inferior good by the **direction of the income effect**, and remember inferior-good demand can *rise* in a recession.
